critic_raw:
This multi-task DL vs. XGBoost cancer treatment response design has a fundamental validity problem with label construction and several significant evaluation gaps.

The multi-label formulation is structurally confounded. Patients are labeled only for treatments they received. In a retrospective cohort, treatment assignment is not random — it is driven by tumor type, genomic profile, patient health status, and clinical guideline adherence. Patients who receive immunotherapy are systematically different from patients who receive chemotherapy. A multi-task model trained on three binary heads (one per treatment) will not be comparing 'what would happen if this patient received treatment X' — it will be learning the outcome distribution conditional on the observed treatment selection mechanism. The baseline XGBoost faces the same confound. Neither model can support causal claims, but more critically, if the goal is actionable treatment selection, neither model's predictions have clear clinical utility: a model that says 'patient responds to immunotherapy' when that patient was selected for immunotherapy based on biomarkers has learned the selection process, not the treatment effect.

Model selection uses validation F1 while primary evaluation uses average precision (AP). These metrics are not aligned: F1 uses a single threshold to compute precision and recall; AP summarizes the precision-recall curve across all thresholds. The best threshold for F1 may not correspond to the best precision-recall operating point, and selecting the model by F1 then evaluating by AP is a criterion mismatch that could favor configurations that are suboptimal for AP.

The genomic risk score — a logistic regression on genomic features fit on training data — is included as a feature and crossed with treatment type. This feature is itself a model, and its construction from training data makes it an implicit ensemble component rather than a raw feature. XGBoost receiving this genomic risk score has access to a pre-trained genomic model's output, which may be better calibrated than the raw genomic features. If the genomic risk score is informative, the comparison tests 'multi-task DL with raw features' against 'XGBoost with an ensemble feature,' which is not a fair architectural comparison.

The stratified random split groups patients by treatment initiation date 'to approximate distribution akin to a temporal setting.' This is not a temporal split. Randomly sampling patients whose initiation dates span 2019-2024 into train/val/test produces sets where the test set contains patients treated before some training patients. In a clinical setting where genomic testing and treatment protocols evolve over 5 years, this allows the model to train on later-protocol patients and test on earlier-protocol patients — the reverse of deployment conditions.

With 10,000 patients and three binary outcome labels per patient (each defined only for the treatment received), the effective sample size per label is much smaller. If 40% of patients receive chemotherapy, 30% immunotherapy, and 30% targeted therapy, each head has ~3,000-4,000 training examples. For deep learning to outperform XGBoost at this scale, the shared representation must provide meaningful transfer across treatment types — but this transfer is precisely what needs to be validated, not assumed.
all_issues_raised: Treatment assignment in the retrospective cohort is confounded by indication — patients labeled for each treatment were selected for that treatment based on biomarkers, so the model learns treatment selection patterns rather than treatment response, making predictions clinically uninterpretable for treatment selection.
Model selection criterion (validation F1) is misaligned with primary evaluation metric (average precision), which can favor configurations suboptimal under the stated evaluation objective.

The genomic risk score included as a feature is itself a model trained on training data, giving XGBoost access to an ensemble feature rather than raw inputs — the comparison is between a multi-task DL model and an implicit ensemble, not equivalent architectures.

The described split is a stratified random sample grouped by treatment date, not a temporal split — test patients can have earlier initiation dates than training patients, violating the deployment-simulation assumption.

Effective sample size per task head (3,000-4,000 examples) is small enough that the deep learning shared representation advantage is an unvalidated assumption, not a demonstrated property, at this data scale.
all_issues_adjudicated: Treatment assignment in the retrospective cohort is confounded by indication — patients labeled for each treatment were selected for that treatment based on biomarkers, so the model learns treatment selection patterns rather than treatment response, making predictions clinically uninterpretable for treatment selection.
Model selection criterion (validation F1) is misaligned with primary evaluation metric (average precision), which can favor configurations suboptimal under the stated evaluation objective.

The genomic risk score included as a feature is itself a model trained on training data, giving XGBoost access to an ensemble feature rather than raw inputs — the comparison is between a multi-task DL model and an implicit ensemble, not equivalent architectures.
